We are told that the patient has a “cough-like-a-seal bark” and high-pitched inspiratory stridor without wheezing—a classic presentation of croup. In croup, subglottic edema causes narrowing of the airway leading to inspiratory stridor and barking cough. Chest X-ray typically shows a “steeple sign,” where the trachea narrows progressively at the level of the subglottis, giving an appearance resembling a steeple. This is due to swelling of the subglottic region. The other choices are either incorrect (increased interstitial markings = pulmonary edema; lobar consolidation = bacterial pneumonia; thumbprint sign = foreign body aspiration) or less specific. C: Thumbprint sign on the lateral image
D: Steeple sign
